questions.question: How does a skip list achieve expected O(log n) without rotations?
options: Layered linked lists where each higher layer is a random ~half-subset of the layer below. A search walks down levels, skipping ~half the remaining nodes at each.
questions.explanation: Level 0 contains all n elements; level k contains each element independently with probability 1/2^k. Expected ~log n levels; expected ~1 step per level → expected O(log n) per op. No balance invariant to maintain — just randomized heights at insert time. Code is ~50 lines (vs ~300 for RB tree).

questions.question: Where does Redis use a skip list in production?
options: SortedSet (ZADD, ZRANGE). Maintains keys ordered by score; skip list provides O(log n) rank queries, range iteration, score updates. Paired with a hash table for O(1) member existence.
questions.explanation: Redis ZADD foo 10 alice / ZADD foo 20 bob / ZRANGE foo 0 -1 needs ordered iteration + O(log n) insert at scored position. antirez chose skip list for: simpler code than RB tree, comparable performance, easier to extend with rank queries. The 'span' field per pointer counts how many bottom-level nodes a link covers — enables O(log n) rank/select. See `src/t_zset.c`.

questions.question: What's the probability that a skip list has max level k+1 for n elements?
options: Each element flips coins until tails. Pr[node level ≥ k] = 1/2^(k-1). Max over n: expected log₂ n, sharply concentrated.
questions.explanation: Level distribution is geometric: Pr[height = k] = 1/2^k. For n elements, the expected number with height ≥ k is n/2^k. Set n/2^k = 1 → k ≈ log₂ n. Concentration: probability max level > 2 log n is exponentially small. In practice cap at ~16 for n ≤ 65K, 32 for huge.

questions.question: Why are skip lists easier than balanced trees to make concurrent (lock-free)?
options: Insert/delete are local pointer-rewires at each level. No rotations cascade up the tree. Lock-free skip list (Herlihy-Shavit) replaces each pointer update with a CAS — much simpler than lock-free RB.
questions.explanation: RB/AVL trees do rotations that move pointers across many nodes simultaneously — lock-free rotations are an open research problem (most concurrent BSTs use locks). Skip lists only update O(log n) pointers locally; CAS each one. Java's ConcurrentSkipListMap and Doug Lea's design are the textbook examples. The cost: skip lists have worse cache behavior than B-trees on modern hardware.

questions.question: How does the skip list 'span' field enable O(log n) rank?
options: Each level-k forward pointer carries `span` = number of level-0 nodes it covers. Sum spans while walking forward to compute the rank of a given key.
questions.explanation: rank(key) returns 'how many keys are ≤ key'. Naïvely O(n). With span field per pointer: when walking forward at level k, you 'skip' span[k] bottom-level nodes. Maintain a running rank counter; total work O(log n). Redis uses this for ZRANK ZREVRANK queries. The span must be updated on insert/delete — about 3x extra arithmetic but worth it.

questions.question: If the random number generator returned all heads, what would happen to a skip list?
options: Every element gets max level → linked list with full towers. O(n) per op. The bad case exists but is exponentially rare under fair randomness. Production cap max level to ~32 to guard against extreme luck.
questions.explanation: Skip list is randomized; the worst case (every node has max height) makes search O(n × max_level). Probability of this happening: 2^(-n × max_level) — basically zero for n > 30. Still, MAX_LEVEL is capped (16-32) to bound memory and guarantee no infinite tower. This is the same 'randomized analysis' as treaps and randomized BSTs — expected guarantees, exponentially rare worst case.
